.\docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:30:A **40× spread on identical work.** So dev is never a single number below — it is modeled as explicit held-box-hours with the §8.4 disciplined-teardown assumption, plus the loose sensitivity.
.\docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:70:The Generator runs on OpenRouter API in every benchmark scenario. The 5-run benchmark is capped at `PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN=40` per run in `smoke.md`; observed 2-LLM smokes ran well under that. Realistic full-4-role benchmark API token spend (Generator + any API-served verifiers) is bounded by **5 runs × ≤$40 = ≤$200 worst case**, typically **$30–$80** for a clean 5-question pass. **This is paid regardless of scenario** (it is the Generator), so it is held constant and excluded from the Vast-vs-Vast comparison below, then added back in §5/§6.
.\docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:76:**Common to all three:** Generator on OpenRouter API; Judge self-host FP8 1×H100; Sentinel self-host A100 80GB EU. Scenarios differ **only in how Mirror is served** — exactly the real decision I-meta-002 faces.
.\docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:78:Dev-hold-hour model (per §0.3, §8.4 disciplined teardown): each self-hosted role gets **~3 debug sessions × ~3 active hrs = ~9 active box-hours**, destroyed between sessions. Integration phase adds **~6 hrs with all self-hosted roles up at once**. Loose-discipline sensitivity (8 hr/day × 10 days held) shown as a flagged upper bound.

.\outputs\codex_findings\m8_review\findings.md:8:- The larger issue is the paused-side state machine. In the current control flow, `JobControl.Paused` exits `runner.run()`, and the worker returns after `mark_paused()`. That means no live executor remains attached to a `paused` job. Under that model, `paused -> running` is not a real transition; it strands the row. The legitimate Phase B resume edge is `paused -> pending`, not `paused -> running`. `paused -> failed` is also not a real runtime edge today unless you add a distinct `resuming` state or worker heartbeat/ownership.
.\outputs\codex_findings\m8_review\findings.md:33:- Change resume to `paused -> pending`; add `paused -> pending` to the allowed graph and remove `paused -> running` unless you also add worker ownership/heartbeat. Consider removing `paused -> failed` too under the current model.

.\outputs\codex_findings\m58_code_audit\findings.md:21:- Blocker: `parse_slot_fill_response` accepts fabricated extracted values as long as the model supplies any real substring for `source_span`. The parser checks `source_span in direct_quote` but never checks that `value` is supported by that span, so `value="1880"` with `source_span="N=1879"` passes and later renders false prose. .\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:1:Frame-driven is the right diagnosis, but your wording is still one layer too polite. The true root cause is not just "custody" and not just "retrieval variance." It is that POLARIS has no mandatory content model for clinical reports. The system treats pivotal trials as optional evidence rows competing inside a generic relevance market. That is why V29 did not move the scoreboard: you fixed transport of evidence inside a corpus-driven architecture, but you did not change the architecture that decides what must exist in the report.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:3:The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:16:On paywalls: yes, this is achievable autonomously up to a ceiling, but not to competitor parity in every clinical dimension. Highest-quality clinical synthesis does require reliable access to primary full text. If the clamp paper or a NEJM/Lancet primary is paywalled and POLARIS cannot legally fetch enough text to verify quantitative claims, then POLARIS cannot honestly match a system whose model already internalized those details or whose product has licensed access. You should stop pretending architecture alone erases that. Architecture can eliminate self-inflicted failure; it cannot conjure inaccessible evidence.
.\outputs\codex_findings\md6_phase1_v1_review\brief.md:8:  - `docs/md6_phase1_threat_model.md` (~190 lines)

One immediate event was reported in the tirzepatide 15-mg group. ¶ Diabetic retinopathy was confirmed by funduscopic examination. Glycemic End Points At 40 weeks, reductions in the mean glycated hemoglobin level with tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg were −2.01 percentage points, −2.24 percentage points, and −2.30 percentage points, respectively, as compared with −1.86 percentage points with semaglutide (Figure 1A). All tirzepatide doses were found to be superior to semaglutide, with estimated treatment differences of −0.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], −0.28 to −0.03; P=0.02) with tirzepatide 5 mg, −0.39 percentage points (95% CI, −0.51 to −0.26; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 10 mg, and −0.45 percentage points (95% CI, −0.57 to −0.32; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 15 mg. The glycated hemoglobin level over time is shown in (Figure 1B). A total of 82 to 86% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 79% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 7.0%. A total of 69 to 80% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 64% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to 6.5% or less, and 27 to 46% and 19%, respectively, had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 5.7% (Fig. 1C). A subgroup analysis involving patients with a glycated hemoglobin level above 8.5% showed mean reductions in the glycated hemoglobin level of −3.22 percentage points with tirzepatide at a dose of 15 mg as compared with −2.68 percentage points with semaglutide (Fig. S8). Figure 1 With the use of the efficacy estimand at 40 weeks, greater reductions in the fasting serum glucose level were observed with all tirzepatide doses than with semaglutide (Figure 1D and Fig. S9). .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:1:Title: Efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis URL Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full Published Time: 2022-10-28 Markdown Content: ## Abstract **Purpose:** A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to combine the data available from clinical trials and evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in people with type 2 diabetes (T2D). **Methods:** We systematically searched the MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, and clinical trials registries ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)) up to 25 March 2022 for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared tirzepatide with placebo or active hypoglycemic drugs in subjects with T2D. Heterogeneity was judged by the _I_ 2 value and Cochran’s Q test. The randomized effects model was adopted to calculate risk ratios and weighted mean differences (WMDs). The primary outcome was the change from baseline in HbA1c levels. Secondary efficacy endpoints were fasting serum glucose (FSG), change of body weight, blood pressure, fasting lipid profiles, and safety indexes. **Results:** Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: −1.44, −0.56), FSG (WMD, −21.50
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:29:diagnosed according to the World Health Organization (1999) or American Diabetes Association (1997) criteria. Reviews, letters, case reports, nonhuman studies, editorials, commentaries, expert opinions, non-RCTs, and meta-analyses were excluded. ### 2.4 Outcome measures of efficacy and safety The change in HbA1c from the baseline was considered the primary outcome of efficacy. The secondary endpoints included proportions of patients with HbA1c 5% of usual body weight (American College of Cardiology/[American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B1)). Our meta-analysis results demonstrated that loss of body weight in the tirzepatide group was from 7.25 kg to 10.36 kg, whether used as monotherapy (7.40 kg) or add-on therapy (8.11 kg). Tirzepatide therapy led to a greater target of the percentage of patients who reached the body weight reduction of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%, whether used as monotherapy or add-on therapy. More interestingly, weight loss was observed at all doses of tirzepatide within 4 weeks after the start of treatment, and this continued until week 40 or 52; no dose of tirzepatide reached a plateau ([Ludvik et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B23); [Rosenstock et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B30)). The effect of tirzepatide on weight loss is expected. The subpopulation of the SURPASS-3 study showed that tirzepatide led to a significant reduction in liver fat content, volume of visceral adipose tissue, and abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue volumes compared with insulin degludec ([Gastaldelli et al., 2022](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B14)). GLP-1 RAs not only show a good hypoglycemic effect but also demonstrate the efficacy of weight loss. A meta-analysis evaluated the weight reduction effects of GLP-1 RAs and exhibited that, compared to placebo, GLP-1 RAs led to significant body weight reduction ([Htike et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B19)). As for semaglutide administered in patients with T2D, compared with placebo, subcutaneous semaglutide led to body weight loss (WMD: -2.73 kg and -4.09 kg, for 0.5 mg and 1 mg, respectively). Oral administration showed similar effects ([Zhong et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B38)). For treatment with dulaglutide in patients with T2D, the mean weight loss was 0.73 kg in the 0.75 mg dulaglutide group and 1.27 kg in the 1.5 mg dulaglutide group ([Qie et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B29)). Our meta-analysis included semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg as the control and presented that tirzepatide reduced body weight greater than the two GLP-1 RAs and resulted in a higher proportion of body weight reaching ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%. Blood pressure and lipid profiles are the risk factors of cardiovasc

.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\evidence_pool.json:7:      "full_text": "Abstract This article reviews the efficacy and safety data of tirzepatide, a once-weekly, novel glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist approved in the United States, the European Union, and other regions for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. All doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators. The safety profile of tirzepatide was consistent with that of the GLP-1 receptor agonist class, with mild to moderate and transient gastrointestinal side effects being the most common adverse events. With clinically and statistically significant reductions in A1C and body weight without increased risk of hypoglycemia in various populations, tirzepatide has demonstrated potential as a first-in-class treatment option for many people with type 2 diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is a metabolic condition caused by increased insulin resistance and progressive \u03b2-cell failure in the pancreas, resulting in decreased insulin secretion and hyperglycemia. Chronic hyperglycemia can ultimately lead to both micro- and macrovascular complications, which can be prevented with appropriate glycemic control. Obesity is a major factor in the pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes; therefore, treatment of type 2 diabetes should address hyperglycemia and comorbidities ([1](#B1),[2](#B2)). Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists are commonly used as first injectable therapy\n\n[...]\n\nrther studies are needed to evaluate these potential mechanisms of action in humans. A phase 2 study assessed the efficacy and safety of four doses (1, 5, 10, and 15 mg) of tirzepatide versus placebo and an active comparator (once-weekly dulaglutide 1.5 mg) in patients with type 2 diabetes ([20](#B20)). The study demonstrated the superiority of tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg versus placebo and dulaglutide 1.5 mg in decreasing A1C at week 26. Likewise, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg were superior to placebo in body weight reduction, and tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were superior to dulaglutide 1.5 mg in body weight reduction. Similar to the tirzepatide phase 1 study ([18](#B18)), most of the tirzepatide adverse events (AEs) were GI-related, with the incidence higher than that for the GLP-1 receptor agonist class. Additionally, a placebo-contr\n\n[...]\n\nfficacy and safety of three doses of tirzepatide (5, 10, and 15 mg) once weekly in participants with type 2 diabetes. Based on the findings of the phase 2 studies, the dose escalation scheme in the SURPASS trials was as follows: all doses started at 2.5 mg once weekly for 4 weeks; then each dose was escalated every 4 weeks in increments of 2.5 mg until the corresponding maintenance dose of tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) was attained and maintained for the remainder of the study ([Figure 1](#F1)). Tirzepatide was compared with placebo as monotherapy in SURPASS-1 ([NCT03954834](https://clini\n\n[...]\n\nblind trials, whereas SURPASS-2, SURPASS-3, and SURPASS-4 were open-label trials. The primary efficacy end point for these studies was the change from baseline in A1C. Efficacy of Tirzepatide Efficacy data presented herein are based on the efficacy estimand, which represents on-treatment ef\n\n[...]\n\n presented in [Table 2](#T2) and [Figure 2A and B](#F2). The A1C reductions were significantly greater in each SURPASS trial for all tirze\n\n[...]\n\nrovascular outcomes (\u223c10 years) ([1](#B1),[27](#B27)). Glycemic values must be personalized based on patient preference, risk of hypoglycemia and other AEs of therapy, and patient comorbidities. The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0%, \u22646.5%, or <5.7% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%, from 66 to 95%, and from 23 to 62%, respectively ([Table 2](#T2)). Significantly more participants achieved the three A1C values with all tirzepatide doses in each of the f\n\n[...]\n\nm initiating therapy. Therefore, broadening the treatment continuum with therapies that achieve glycemic control with weight loss effects is important. In the SURPASS trials, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from \u22126.2 kg (\u22126.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to \u221212.9 kg (\u221213.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3) ([Table 2](#T2) and [Figure 2B](#F2)). In each of the SURPASS clinical trials, body weight reductions were significantly greater for all tirzepatide dose\n\n[...]\n\nS studies compared with placebo or active comparators at the primary end point. There was an overall improvement in the fasting lipid profile with tirzepatide across the SURPASS trials. Namely, there was a decrease from baseline in triglycerides (by 15.2\u221226.7%), LDL cholesterol (by 5.2\u221215.5%), and VLDL cholesterol (by 14.2\u221225.2%) and an increase from baseline in HDL cholesterol (by 0.9\u221210.8%) with all tirzepatide doses at the primary end point. Decreases in systolic (of 2.8\u221212.6 mmHg) and diastolic (of 0.8\u22124.5 mmHg) blood pressure were also observed with tirzepatide in all SURPASS trials. .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\evidence_pool.json:23:      "full_text": "Title: Efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis URL Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full Published Time: 2022-10-28 Markdown Content: ## Abstract **Purpose:** A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to combine the data available from clinical trials and evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in people with type 2 diabetes (T2D). **Methods:** We systematically searched the MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, and clinical trials registries ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)) up to 25 March 2022 for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared tirzepatide with placebo or active hypoglycemic drugs in subjects with T2D. Heterogeneity was judged by the _I_ 2 value and Cochran\u2019s Q test. The randomized effects model was adopted to calculate risk ratios and weighted mean differences (WMDs). The primary outcome was the change from baseline in HbA1c levels. Secondary efficacy endpoints were fasting serum glucose (FSG), change of body weight, blood pressure, fasting lipid profiles, and safety indexes. **Results:** Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: \u22121.44, \u22120.56), FSG (WMD, \u221221.50\n\n[...]\n\nty indexes. **Results:** Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: \u22121.44, \u22120.56), FSG (WMD, \u221221.50 mg/dl; 95% CI: \u221234.44, \u22128.56), body weight (WMD: \u22127.99 kg; 95% CI \u221211.36, \u22124.62), and blood pressure and ameliorated fasting lipid profiles, without increasing hypoglycemia, either as monotherapy or an add-on therapy. Tirzepatide increased the risk of gastrointestinal adverse events mainly in add-on therapy but not in terms of\n\n[...]\n\netes has become one of the most prevalent non-communicable chronic diseases, resulting in disabilities, expensive complications, and even shortening of life expectancy ([Saeedi et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B32)). Unfortunately, in the world, the prevalence rate of diabetes in the population of age 20\u201379 years was 10.5% in 2021, which was about 536 million 600 thousand; by 2045, it is expected to increase up to 12.2%, which will be about 783 million 200 thousand ([Sun H. et al., 2022](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B37)). According to the pathophysiology of diabetes, different antihyperglycemic drugs have been developed for clinical application. Since human insulin was approved in 1982, the FDA has approved 59 drugs for controlling hyperglycemia ([Dahlen et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B6)). Among these antihyperglycemic agents, incretin-dependent T2D therapies play an important role. As a 30-amino-acid-peptide, glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) exerts biological effects as an incretin-stimulating hormone. The first batch of in\n\n[...]\n\ne used increasingly and show excellent clinical benefits including decreased glucose and body weight, lowered cardiovascular risk, and reduced risk of hypoglycemia ([Lyseng-Williamson, 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B24)). Glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP), the other incretin hormone, is similar to GLP-1 and its receptor; however, GIP does not inhibit appetite and food intake ([Holst and Rosenkilde, 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B18)). Thus, it is assumed that the combination of GLP-1 RA with other drugs acting on GIP receptors may produce more effective blood glucose control and weight loss. Tirzepatide, a dual GIP and GLP-1 RA, is a polypeptide containing 39 amino ac\n\n[...]\n\n sequence, and conjugated with a fatty acid chain similar to the semaglutide side chain to promote its binding with albumin, so as to prolong the half-life of the drug ([Coskun et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B4)). A series of clinical trials on tirzepatide have explored its efficacy and safety in the treatment of T2D and shown great potential in decreasing hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) and body weight ([Min and Bain, 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B27)). There are two systematic reviews and meta-analyses on the evaluation of the effectiveness of tirzepatide on the treatment of diabetes ([Bhagavathula et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B2); [Dutta et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B10)). They both included one randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial which appraised the efficacy and tolerability of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes lasting for 12 weeks ([Frias et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B12)). However, the dose regimens in the study were dose-escalation. The duration of each dose was 2\u20134 weeks, and the treatment time of tirzepatide at the target stable dose was only 4 weeks, which was too short to judge the efficacy of a speci\n\n[...]\n\nsponse analysis. Finally, the results of SURPASS-5 were published in 2022 and were not included. We performed and updated a systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the safety and efficacy of tirzepatide in patients with T2D. ## 2 Methods ### 2.1 Protocol Our systematic review and meta-analysis was executed and reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement ([Page et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B28)). The protocol was registered in PROSPERO (No. CRD42022319442). ### 2.2 Search strategy The Embase, MEDLINE, and Cochrane databases and clinical trials registries ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)) were comprehensively searched prior to 25 March 2022, without limitations on language, race, or co\n\n[...]\n\nn OR dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist) AND (type 2 diabetes OR diabetes) AND (randomized controlled trial). The search strategy was adjusted to meet the requirements of each database. ### 2.3 Inclusion and exclusion criteria We included randomized controlled trials (RCTs), which lasted at least 12 weeks, that assessed and compared the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide with other hypoglycemic agents or placebos in T2D patients (\u226518 years\n\n[...]\n\ndiagnosed according to the World Health Organization (1999) or American Diabetes Association (1997) criteria. Reviews, letters, case reports, nonhuman studies, editorials, commentaries, expert opinions, non-RCTs, and meta-analyses were excluded. ### 2.4 Outcome measures of efficacy and safety The change in HbA1c from the baseline was considered the primary outcome of efficacy. The secondary endpoints included proportions of patients with HbA1c 5% of usual body weight (American College of Cardiology/[American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B1)). Our meta-analysis results demonstrated that loss of body weight in the tirzepatide group was from 7.25 kg to 10.36 kg, whether used as monotherapy (7.40 kg) or add-on therapy (8.11 kg). Tirzepatide therapy led to a greater target of the percentage of patients who reached the body weight reduction of \u22655%, \u226510%, and \u226515%, whether used as monotherapy or add-on therapy. More interestingly, weight loss was observed at all doses of tirzepatide within 4 weeks after the start of treatment, and this continued until week 40 or 52; no dose of tirzepatide reached a plateau ([Ludvik et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B23); [Rosenstock et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B30)). The effect of tirzepatide on weight loss is expected. The subpopulation of the SURPASS-3 study showed that tirzepatide led to a significant reduction in liver fat content, volume of visceral adipose tissue, and abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue volumes compared with insulin degludec ([Gastaldelli et al., 2022](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B14)). GLP-1 RAs not only show a good hypoglycemic effect but also demonstrate the efficacy of weight loss. A meta-analysis evaluated the weight reduction effects of GLP-1 RAs and exhibited that, compared to placebo, GLP-1 RAs led to significant body weight reduction ([Htike et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B19)). As for semaglutide administered in patients with T2D, compared with placebo, subcutaneous semaglutide led to body weight loss (WMD: -2.73 kg and -4.09 kg, for 0.5 mg and 1 mg, respectively). Oral administration showed similar effects ([Zhong et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B38)). For treatment with dulaglutide in patients with T2D, the mean weight loss was 0.73 kg in the 0.75 mg dulaglutide group and 1.27 kg in the 1.5 mg dulaglutide group ([Qie et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B29)). Our meta-analysis included semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg as the control and presented that tirzepatide reduced body weight greater than the two GLP-1 RAs and resulted in a higher proportion of body weight reaching \u22655%, \u226510%, and \u226515%. Blood pressure and lipid profiles are the risk factors of cardiovasc",

Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes Published June 25, 2021 N Engl J Med 2021;385:503-515 DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2107519 Abstract Background Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that is under development for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. The efficacy and safety of once-weekly tirzepatide as compared with semaglutide, a selective GLP-1 receptor agonist, are unknown. Methods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was \u22122.01 percentage points, \u22122.24 percentage points, and \u22122.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and \u22121.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-\n\n[...]\n\nMethods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was \u22122.01 percentage points, \u22122.24 percentage points, and \u22122.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and \u22121.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-mg tirzepatide groups and the semaglutide group were \u22120.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], \u22120.28 to \u22120.03; P=0.02), \u22120.39 percentage points (95% CI, \u22120.51 to \u22120.26; P 8.5% [\u226469 or >69 mmol per mol]). Tirzepatide was initiated at a dose of 2.5 mg once weekly, and the doses were increased by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks until the randomly assigned dose was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial. Semaglutide was initiated at a dose of 0.25 mg once weekly, and the dose was doubled every 4 weeks until 1 mg was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial.20 Dose de-escalation was not allowed. The initiation of new antihyperglycemic medications was allowed ac\n\n[...]\n\neme, described in the Statistical Analyses Methods section in the Supplementary Appendix, Figs. S2 through S6, and Table S2) were the change in body weight from baseline to week 40 and the attainment of glycated hemoglobin level targets of less than 7.0% and less than 5.7%. Tirzepatide is an investigational once-weekly, dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist that integrates the actions of both incretins into a single molecule. The multicenter, randomized, double-blind, parallel, placebo-controlled SURMOUNT-3 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04657016](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04657016?term=NCT04657016&rank=1)) evaluated the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for 72 weeks after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention lead-in period in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities, excluding type 2 diabetes. At the end of the 12-week period, study participants (N=579) achieved 6.9% mean weight loss and were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive tirzepatide or placebo subcutaneously once weekly. The efficacy estimand (efficacy prior to discontinuation of study drug) results demonstrated that treatment with tirzepatide was associated with an additional 21.1% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 3.3% weight regain for the placebo group. Over the 72 weeks, 94.4% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 10.7% of those receiving placebo. Over the 72 weeks, 94.4% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 10.7% of those receiving placebo. The total mean body weight reduction for patients treated with tirzepatide from study entry after 12 weeks of lifestyle intervention to 72 weeks of treatment was 26.6%. The treatment-regimen estimand (estimated average treatment effect regardless of treatment discontinuation) results demonstrated that tirzepatide was associated with an additional 18.4% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 2.5% regain with placebo. Moreover, 87.5% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 16.5% of those in the placebo arm. The total mean body weight reduction for patients treated with tirzepatide from study entry after 12 weeks of lifestyle intervention to 72 weeks of treatment was 24.3%. .\src\polaris_graph\audit_bundle\REVIEWER_README.md:15:- `reasoning_trace.jsonl` — raw model reasoning channel, one record per generator LLM call (see "Reasoning-trace note" below). Process evidence, **NOT** verified claims.
.\src\polaris_graph\audit_bundle\REVIEWER_README.md:43:`reasoning_trace.jsonl` records the **raw model reasoning channel** for every generator LLM call in the run — one JSON object per completed provider response. DeepSeek V4 Pro is reasoning-first: it emits a large internal reasoning trace alongside its final answer. POLARIS captures that trace here, stored **separately** from the report.
.\src\polaris_graph\audit_bundle\REVIEWER_README.md:45:**This is model-process evidence, NOT verified claims.**
.\src\polaris_graph\audit_bundle\REVIEWER_README.md:48:- The reasoning trace is the model *thinking out loud* — it may contain discarded hypotheses, scratch arithmetic, or self-corrections. Do not cite it as a source.
.\src\polaris_graph\audit_bundle\REVIEWER_README.md:50:- When `content_source = promoted_from_reasoning`, the model returned an empty content channel and POLARIS used the reasoning channel as the answer (I-bug-088 response-shape recovery) — `reasoning_text` and `content_text` legitimately overlap there; the record discloses it rather than hiding it.

.\src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:715:13. **Policy-scope disambiguation (M-NEW-1, GH#422)**: When a paragraph names a specific program (Bill C-64, ACA, MACRA, EU AI Act, Article 34.7 CUSMA review, a particular budget line, etc.) and the evidence pool also contains projections / cost estimates / impact analyses for a RELATED-BUT-BROADER scope (universal single-payer projection vs phase-1 narrow program; comprehensive coverage estimate vs narrow amendment; multi-jurisdiction equivalent of a single-state rule), do NOT silently fold the broader projection into the narrow-program paragraph. When citing numbers from the broader scope, EXPLICITLY label the scope-attribution INLINE in the SAME sentence as the citation. Required pattern: write "PBO 2023 universal single-payer projection estimates the additional cost at $11.2B in 2024-25 [ev_X]" — NOT "the incremental cost is $11.2B in 2024-25 [ev_X]" inside a paragraph that opens with Bill C-64 phase-1 (which covers only contraception and diabetes medications). The decimal and the citation are correct; the missing scope label is what makes the conflation. Same evidence-ID, additional 4-8 word scope phrase before the citation. This rule fires regardless of which section the named-program paragraph appears in (Regulatory, Comparative, Economic, etc.). Failure mode this rule prevents: a reader concludes a narrow program will cost the broader program's projected figure. Concrete examples (placeholders only): GOOD: "Bill C-64 covers a defined set of contraception and diabetes medications [ev_A]. The PBO's 2023 cost estimate of a universal single-payer drug plan modeled on an expanded Quebec formulary projects incremental public-sector cost of $11.2B in 2024-25 [ev_B]." — distinct scopes named, decimals attributed to broader scope. BAD: "Under Bill C-64, the incremental cost to the public sector is estimated at $11.2 billion in 2024-25 [ev_B]." — cites the PBO universal-plan source as if it were a Bill C-64 phase-1 projection.

.\tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:5:Weight reduction is the most consistently observed outcome across IF trials, and it is mechanistically central to the improvement of insulin resistance in T2DM. In adults with overweight or obesity, IF reduced fat mass by a mean difference of −0.72 kg compared with a non-intervention diet [CITE:ev_243de27dc515c169]. When IF is combined with structured physical exercise, the weight reduction relative to a control diet plus exercise reaches a weighted mean difference of −1.83 kg (P = 0.001) [CITE:ev_749f00cb5c5eea28], suggesting an additive effect when caloric timing is paired with energy expenditure. The largest effect size is observed with modified alternate-day fasting, which produced a weight reduction of −5.18 kg (95% CI: −7.04 to −3.32) compared with a usual diet, supported by high certainty of evidence [CITE:ev_e7bdb4797d3f6f0f]. The differential between these protocols is clinically meaningful: a five-kilogram loss falls within the range associated with measurable improvements in HbA1c and insulin sensitivity, whereas sub-kilogram fat-mass reductions, although statistically significant, may yield more modest glycemic benefits.

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_4_phase_0a_0\D5_prompt_family_inventory.md:57:1. **Reachable under the FROZEN gold-set configuration (Codex round-1 #4)**: reachability is defined under the frozen production config used for gold-set generation+verification — entry points, env vars, model slugs, provider-fallback policy, feature flags, router config, retry settings, enabled gen/verifier stages. The call graph is too dynamic for static enumeration ALONE (router decisions, retries, model branches, fallback LLMs, data-dependent blocks). Static tracing SEEDS the inventory; **instrumented dry-run coverage is the ENFORCEMENT surface**.
.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_4_phase_0a_0\D5_prompt_family_inventory.md:59:3. **Per-constructor enumeration**: a single file may host MANY constructors (e.g. `multi_section_generator.py` — outline / section / trial-table / limitations / per-trial-subsection / retry / model-conditioned). Enumeration is per-CONSTRUCTOR (per-skeleton), not per-file.
.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_4_phase_0a_0\D5_prompt_family_inventory.md:60:4. **Call-boundary instrumentation + completeness fail-closed (Codex round-1 #5)**: the invariant "every emitted prompt resolves to an enumerated family else halt" is enforced AT THE LLM CALL BOUNDARY, not by reverse-mapping prompt text. The central call wrapper (e.g. `OpenRouterClient.generate`) receives/derives `{role, family_key, prompt_family_id, system_template_id, user_template_id, retry_path, model_conditioned_branch, callable_qualname}`, validates against the active `prompt_families.json`, and writes the `prompt_family_id` into the prompt trace + construction_manifest. An emitted prompt that does not resolve to an active family is fail-closed (halt).

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_4_phase_0a_0\codex_trail\d8_review.txt:8:The third blocker is the resample-vs-expand rule. The target-band concept is right if it means “the DEFF/raw-N assumption used for §3.2 sizing,” but the current “RESAMPLE OR expand raw N” leaves an unregistered choice. Fix with a deterministic rule, for example: accept the first seed draw whose per-stratum DEFF is within the pre-registered upper bound; resample up to K attempts using sequential draw indexes; if still out-of-band, expand raw N for the failing stratum to `ceil(n_eff_required × observed_DEFF)` or trigger the defined §P4 Category-3 amendment. Also define whether the lower bound matters for representativeness or whether only the upper bound is binding for safety.

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\04_v3_2_review.txt:708:Logit-ANOVA is defensible as a transparent pre-outcome surrogate audit, but beta-binomial or logistic-normal random-intercept GLMM is more canonical for count-over-denominator proportions, especially with unequal denominators, 0/1 proportions, and overdispersion. .\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\03_v3_1_review.txt:743:The three surrogates are defensible, with source-disagreement frequency the best primary candidate because it is closest to missed-fabrication risk. Evidence completeness and retrieval-tier prevalence are useful but more process-proxy than outcome-proxy. I would add one verifier-native pre-outcome surrogate, such as unsupported-claim score, verifier escalation rate, fallback rate, or verifier confidence dispersion, because customer clustering may enter through verifier behavior rather than retrieval alone. The escalation rule is conservative enough, but two implementation details need tightening: define the one-sided ICC CI method, and replace `ceil(max_observed × 1.5)` with “round up to nearest 0.01 or 0.05.” Mathematical `ceil(0.11 × 1.5)` equals 1.0, which is almost certainly not intended. For proportion surrogates, predefine weighting or a mixed/binomial method because one-way ANOVA on raw proportions can be heteroskedastic.
.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\03_v3_1_review.txt:749:The `{1%, 5%, 10%, 30%}` grid is useful for version comparison, but it should not be the only standardized grid unless production S0/S1 prevalence is plausibly in that range. For safety-critical events, production prevalence may be below 1%, where PPV can collapse even with strong recall/specificity. Add a production-centered log grid, for example `{0.1%, 0.5%, 1%, 2%, 5%, 10%, 30%}`, plus the observed canary prevalence and its CI. Binding on production-canary prevalence is correct, but the PPV interval should propagate uncertainty in both classifier performance and prevalence. Predictive values are prevalence-dependent, so the explicit labeling is methodologically necessary. ([bmj.com](https://www.bmj.com/content/329/7458/168?utm_source=openai))
.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\03_v3_1_review.txt:755:The expansion is strong, but evaluation-time leakage should be explicit. Add a subsection for LLM-as-judge and evaluator leakage: judge model, judge prompt, rubric, exemplars, tool/RAG index, memory, cached rationales, and evaluator traces must not be trained, tuned, selected, or revised using gold-set artifacts or descendants. Also require provider-side data-retention/training opt-out for any external judge API, frozen judge model snapshot, and no use of judge outputs to revise prompts before the gate is complete. This is partly covered by items 18 and 19-31, but not tightly enough for LLM evaluation. Recent LLM benchmark work treats test contamination and judge-related bias as first-order evaluation risks. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.01534)) ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.19314))
.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\03_v3_1_review.txt:761:The table is tight but needs a global anti-overclaim layer. Add forbidden language for: “zero hallucinations,” “clinically safe,” “production-wide bound,” “95% overall confidence,” “regulatory-grade validation,” “all future versions,” and “all domains/customers” unless independently supported. Gate A and B pass sentences should parameterize the ICC ceiling as `0.10 or escalated value X`, not hard-code 0.10. Gate C should mention sparse-cell rollups or non-tested cells when present. Gate D should say classifier-based gating is eligible only in passing cells and that precision is workload, not safety. P1/P2 failure language should be mandatory anywhere Gate A is cited. Also require every pass sentence to name the model/prompt/index version and monitoring window.

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\02_v3_0_review.txt:548:For audit-grade language, I would make BY the default unless a pre-registered simulation or null-resampling analysis supports BH. If you want a switch rule, base it on test statistics or null residuals, not observed p-values. For example: build baseline-period null resamples preserving report/customer/cell structure, estimate the cell-level test-statistic correlation matrix, and permit BH only if dependence is negligible or demonstrably positive/PRDS-like under the locked testing procedure.
.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\02_v3_0_review.txt:558:I would also report gold-set prevalence precision, but only with an explicit non-safety label: “validation-set/enriched-prevalence precision.” It is useful for debugging and for comparing model versions on the same validation construction, but it is not a production PPV and should not appear in readiness claims.

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\02_v3_0_review.txt:578:Finally, distinguish allowed production evidence from forbidden constructed holdout artifacts. Some source documents may legitimately live in the production retrieval corpus. The contamination rule should prohibit using holdout claim labels, constructed scenarios, templates, source packets as packets, and known contradiction annotations for model/prompt development, while allowing ordinary retrieval from the locked production corpus when that is the estimand.

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\00_deep_dialogue_v2_response.txt:369:The zero-event bootstrap problem is real. .\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\00_deep_dialogue_v2_response.txt:412:The important part is “not declared passing.” Sparse cells should not silently inherit confidence from aggregate performance unless you pre-register pooling. .\state\polaris_statistical_contract\v3_3\codex_review_trail\00_deep_dialogue_v2_response.txt:452:For two raters and nominal categories, Cohen’s κ is standard. For multiple raters with varying raters per item, Cohen’s κ is not enough. You would look at Fleiss’ κ, Krippendorff’s α, or a model-based agreement approach. For ordinal severity classes, weighted κ or ordinal versions of agreement are often better because confusing S0 with S1 is not the same as confusing S0 with S3.

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\human_free_amendment\AMENDMENT_human_free_validation.md:13:v3.3 + the Phase-0a.0 deliverables put **human SMEs** in load-bearing roles: claim construction, blinded "is it fabricated?" labeling, severity adjudication, tiebreak, and Gate E (human inter-rater reliability — the validity gate that makes Gates A/B/D interpretable). The no-human constraint removes all of these. This amendment replaces them with a **mechanical-oracle + deterministic-hazard + cross-family-LLM** model and **re-scopes the safety claim accordingly**.
.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\human_free_amendment\AMENDMENT_human_free_validation.md:22:- POLARIS **cannot** make the **severity / risk stratum** (clinical or non-clinical) ground truth without humans. It can make severity a **machine-adjudicated risk stratum** (structured-rule-constrained + cross-family-LLM-adjudicated). "Clinical" is reserved for the clinical domain only; the benchmark spans 6 domains, so the generic term is **severity/risk stratum** (Codex P2 — terminology consistency).

.\state\polaris_statistical_contract\human_free_amendment\codex_trail\04_contract_convert_iter1.txt:819:state\compare_gemini_q1.md:7:I've completed your research. Feel free to ask me follow-up questions or request changes.Canada AI Compute: Sovereign vs. HyperscalerThe 2026 AI Compute Trilemma: Navigating Cost, Quality, and Jurisdiction in Canada's Federal AI WorkloadsIntroduction: The Sovereign Compute Imperative in 2026As artificial intelligence transitions from a frontier experimental capability to a foundational element of public infrastructure, the Canadian federal government is confronting a profound architectural and policy trilemma. The rapid deployment of advanced large language models (LLMs) requires infrastructure that simultaneously satisfies rigorous data sovereignty requirements, delivers world-class computational performance, and remains economically viable in an era of escalating energy and hardware costs. In 2026, this dynamic is encapsulated by the strategic choice between cultivating a domestic, sovereign AI compute ecosystem—anchored by massive federal initiatives like the Artificial Intelligence Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP) and industry clusters like SCALE AI—versus leveraging the immense scale, ecosystem maturity, and localized cryptographic adaptations of United States-headquartered hyperscalers such as Microsoft Azure, Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Google Cloud Platform (GCP).The stakes in 2026 extend far beyond the mechanics of public procurement; they encompass national security, economic competitiveness, and the absolute preservation of domestic intellectual property. The Canadian government has explicitly designated digital sovereignty as a paramount objective, aiming to exercise autonomy over digital infrastructure, proprietary data, and critical technologies while aggressively insulating federal operations from extraterritorial legal frameworks. Concurrently, the global demand for AI compute has reached staggering proportions. By the end of 2025, total global AI data center power capacity exceeded 30 gigawatts—a figure comparable to the peak power usage of entire industrialized states such as New York, and substantially larger than the baseline demand of many developed nations. Within this constrained and fiercely competitive global market, Canada has launched a $2 billion Sovereign AI Compute Strategy to secure domestic capacity, committing approximately $890 million to the Infrastructure Build Layer of SCIP alone.   However, global hyperscalers have not remained static in the face of these protectionist shifts. Recognizing the vulnerability of their lucrative public sector market share to rising data sovereignty concerns, firms like Microsoft have countered with historic investments. Microsoft's unprecedented $19 billion commitment to Canada introduces localized cloud controls, confidential computing enclaves, and contractual legal shields designed to seamlessly mimic the benefits of physical sovereignty while retaining global cloud agility. Furthermore, the fundamental economic foundations of Canadian computing are undergoing a paradigm shift. Historically heavily reliant on abundant, inexpensive hydroelectric power, jurisdictions like Quebec are sharply increasing tariffs for energy-intensive AI data centers to manage grid stability and protect residential consumers. This pricing evolution fundamentally alters the unit economics of domestic model training, eroding the long-standing geographic arbitrage that previously favored Canadian deployments.   This comprehensive report exhaustively analyzes the cost-quality-jurisdiction trade-off inherent in Canada's federal AI compute strategy. By meticulously examining the legal mechanisms of extraterritorial data access under United States law, the shifting economics of frontier hardware like the NVIDIA B200 Blackwell architecture, the severe impact of provincial power rate adjustments, and the software stack maturity of competing computing ecosystems, this analysis provides a definitive framework for optimizing federal AI workloads across strict Protected B and Protected C security classifications.The Jurisdictional Imperative and the Architecture of Data SovereigntyThe foundational tension driving the federal compute procurement debate is the definition, enforcement, and technological realization of data sovereignty. For the Government of Canada, digital sovereignty is not merely a political aspiration but a strict, legally binding operational requirement, particularly governed by bodies such as Shared Services Canada (SSC) and informed by legislative updates including Bill C-26 and the Critical Cyber Systems Protection Act. The classification of federal data dictates the stringency of these sovereignty controls. Protected B encompasses information that could cause serious injury to an individual or government organization if compromised, such as detailed financial information or medical records. Protected C represents the highest tier of non-classified sensitivity, encompassing information that could cause extremely grave injury, including national security intelligence, advanced defense logistics, and highly sensitive diplomatic communications.   The Extraterritorial Reach of the US CLOUD ActThe primary catalyst for Canada's aggressive sovereign compute push is the United States Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data (CLOUD) Act of 2018. The CLOUD Act fundamentally altered the international data jurisdiction landscape by establishing that United States law enforcement authorities can compel US-based service providers to disclose data regardless of where that data is physically stored around the globe. For federal planners, this legislation laid bare the distinction between mere data residency and genuine data sovereignty.   The application of the CLOUD Act to Canadian data operates through three distinct and pervasive legal pathways. The first is direct application; when Canadian entities utilize services provided directly by US-headquartered corporations, the data is subject to US jurisdiction via the parent company's geographic domicile. The second, and most legally complex, is application through corporate control. The CLOUD Act's "possession, custody, or control" doctrine extends the legislation's reach to data held by foreign subsidiaries of US companies. This means that a Canadian-incorporated subsidiary of a global hyperscaler is still legally bound to comply with United States judicial orders, rendering local incorporation an insufficient shield. The third pathway involves application to foreign companies with a US presence; any service provider with a sufficient operational footprint in the US, regardless of its global headquarters, may be subject to the Act's sweeping provisions.   For federal departments operating under the mandate of Shared Services Canada (SSC), this sweeping extraterritoriality creates a severe and unacceptable compliance vulnerability. The SSC has explicitly stated in policy white papers that reliance on hyperscale cloud service providers (CSPs) with foreign operations means Canada simply cannot ensure full sovereignty over its data. This exposes sensitive government assets to foreign laws and potential disclosure to a foreign government, potentially without the Government of Canada's prior knowledge or consent.   This is not a hypothetical academic risk. It is the operational reality of utilizing foreign infrastructure. When a senior Microsoft executive was asked under oath before the French Senate in June 2025 whether he could guarantee that French government data stored in Microsoft's cloud would never be transmitted to US authorities without French authorization, his answer was unequivocal: "Non, je ne peux pas le garantir" ("No, I cannot guarantee it"). The identical legal reality applies to Canadian federal data. Storing data on Canadian soil does not automatically protect it; the CLOUD Act compels disclosure based on who ultimately controls the data, not strictly where the server racks are physically bolted down.   While data residency ensures physical storage within Canada, the US CLOUD Act asserts jurisdiction based on corporate control. True digital sovereignty requires the infrastructure operator to be exclusively subject to Canadian law, insulating data from extraterritorial legal demands. The architectural distinction is stark: a hyperscaler framework permits a foreign legal order to bypass physical geographic borders via corporate parent-subsidiary hierarchies, whereas a fully sovereign architecture halts such jurisdictional overreach directly at the national border due to independent, air-gapped domestic ownership. Encryption at rest, while an industry-standard security baseline, does not mitigate this specific jurisdictional risk if the cloud provider retains the ability to access the decryption keys or view the decrypted data during active computational processing.   The Hyperscaler Response: Cryptographic Sovereignty and Market DefenseRecognizing the existential threat that strict data sovereignty mandates pose to their massive public sector market share, United States hyperscalers have heavily criticized policies that conflate geographic server location with genuine security and resilience. Prominent think tanks and industry analysts argue that the risk of foreign legal orders is a theoretical extremity that ignores the practical, empirical reality of modern cloud security. Public transparency reports published by AWS, Microsoft, and Google indicate zero instances of Canadian enterprise or government content being disclosed to US law enforcement under the CLOUD Act, despite tens of thousands of routine global law enforcement demands.   To address the jurisdictional gap without fracturing their highly integrated global network architectures, hyperscalers have pioneered a concept that can be termed "cryptographic sovereignty." In 2026, Microsoft launched its Sovereign AI Landing Zone (SAIL) in Canada, backed by an unprecedented $19 billion capital investment pledge. This advanced architecture utilizes Azure Confidential Compute, a technology that keeps data strictly encrypted and isolated at the hardware level even while it is actively being processed in memory by AI models. Coupled with external key management via Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) that remain entirely under the Canadian client's control, hyperscalers assert that they are mathematically and physically incapable of complying with a foreign subpoena because they lack the cryptographic means to access the plaintext data, thereby neutralizing the CLOUD Act's reach.   Furthermore, hyperscalers have instituted rigorous organizational and contractual controls tailored specifically for the Canadian federal government. Google Cloud's Data Boundary for the Government of Canada ensures that technical support for Protected B workloads is provided exclusively by Canadian personnel who have completed extensive Reliability Status security screenings. The supported cloud services have successfully completed the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (CCCS) rigorous assessment to support Protected B Medium (PBMM) and Protected B High Value Asset (PBHVA) workloads.   Amazon Web Services (AWS) offers similar verifiable controls over data access, combined with strict, codified contractual commitments to legally challenge any inappropriate or overbroad foreign data requests. AWS emphasizes that the CLOUD Act does not prohibit technical measures and operational controls designed to prevent access to customer data, asserting that their technical controls create multiple layers of protection that operate independently of corporate jurisdiction.   Despite these highly advanced technical mitigations, cryptographic sovereignty remains a heavily debated substitute for absolute legal sovereignty. As long as the physical infrastructure and underlying hypervisor are operated by a United States entity, theoretical attack vectors—or unprecedented extraterritorial legal maneuvers compelling the alteration of the host operating environment itself—remain a nonzero risk. For the most critical Protected C AI workloads, where the risk tolerance is functionally zero, this dependency renders the hyperscaler approach highly contentious.   The Domestic Ecosystem: SCALE AI and the Sovereign Compute StrategyTo establish a definitive, legally impregnable alternative to foreign CSPs, the Government of Canada initiated the $2 billion Canadian Sovereign AI Compute Strategy. This strategy is an aggressive, interventionist industrial policy designed to rectify the systemic underinvestment in domestic infrastructure that previously forced promising Canadian AI startups to incorporate abroad or rely entirely on United States infrastructure to achieve scale.   The AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP)The centerpiece of this federal strategy is the AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP), a highly structured initiative designed to construct a massive, publicly accessible AI supercomputer. Backed by approximately $890 million directed explicitly toward the Infrastructure Build Layer, SCIP represents a coordinated national effort to repatriate compute capacity and anchor a secure ecosystem.   A critical element of SCIP is its uncompromising sovereignty criteria, explicitly drafted to counter the CLOUD Act. Section 6 of the program guidelines strictly mandates that core compute and storage infrastructure must be owned or contractually controlled by Canadian entities, implementing robust safeguards to ensure no foreign party can unilaterally restrict use or demand access. The program insists that Canadian data, including highly sensitive datasets, must have appropriate protections while being leveraged to build AI solutions that directly address Canadian needs.   The SCIP architecture is deliberately bifurcated into two integrated operational layers to ensure the hardware is effectively utilized by the broader innovation ecosystem:The Infrastructure Build Layer: This component is responsible for the physical design, construction, systems administration, hardware operations, and long-term data center management.   The National Service Layer: This layer is tasked with transforming "bare metal" hardware into a highly usable platform. This includes providing the user interface, extensive APIs, AI and machine learning compute-as-a-service frameworks, active data management repositories, and seamless integration with Canada's existing Digital Research Infrastructure (DRI) ecosystem.   The Role of SCALE AI and Digital ClustersComplementing the raw infrastructure build is the deployment of specialized funding and industry integration through national clusters. Scale AI, Canada's AI-Powered Supply Chains Cluster based in Montréal, operates under a co-investment model that bridges the gap between Canada's world-class AI research and its commercial deployment. By late 2025, Scale AI announced a historic, record-breaking $128.5 million CAD funding round to support 44 new applied AI projects, driving collective investments toward the $500 million mark.   The critical sovereignty aspect of Scale AI's operations is its strict retention of intellectual property. As of March 2025, 100% of the IP created in the cluster's first 100 projects remained Canadian-owned, ensuring that the algorithmic innovations generated domestically are not immediately absorbed by foreign conglomerates. Projects funded range from automating municipal infrastructure management to complex port logistics optimization tools, demonstrating the broad utility of the ecosystem. Concurrently, the Vancouver-based Digital Global Innovation Cluster (DIGITAL) is advancing Canada's leadership in emerging digital technologies, injecting further capital into collaborative, industry-led projects.   Private Sector Alignment: TELUS and Alpha ComputeThe federal strategy is further amplified by massive private-sector investments explicitly aligning with the sovereign mandate. The telecommunications giant TELUS, in direct collaboration with the Government of Canada, has pioneered a network of Sovereign AI Factories. Following the rapid, unprecedented sell-out of its initial facility in Rimouski, Quebec, TELUS is executing a massive expansion into British Columbia with a cluster projected to scale to an astonishing 60,000 high-performance GPUs and 150 megawatts of power demand by 2032. This project represents a $9 billion economic injection and is explicitly marketed as a secure alternative to global hyperscalers. TELUS guarantees that intellectual property and sensitive data will never traverse outside Canadian borders, while running the entire operation on 98% clean hydroelectric energy and utilizing advanced liquid cooling with heat recovery technologies.   Additionally, highly agile domestic infrastructure firms like Alpha Compute have demonstrated the capability to deploy frontier hardware at speeds rivaling global tech giants. In early 2026, Alpha Compute officially activated the ALPHA-01 cluster in a Canadian datacenter, featuring 504 of the latest NVIDIA B200 GPUs, with aggressive plans to scale beyond 1,000 GPUs using specialized, hardware-backed non-recourse debt financing. These deployments comprehensively prove that Canadian sovereign clusters are not relegated to utilizing trailing-edge or outdated hardware; they are successfully securing tier-one supply chain allocations alongside global competitors.   The Cost Trade-Off: Hardware Economics, Power Tariffs, and the Energy ShockOperating sovereign frontier AI compute involves immense, multi-generational capital expenditure (CapEx) and deeply complex operational expenditure (OpEx). While the federal SCIP funding and private debt markets provide the initial CapEx necessary to secure hardware, the OpEx of Canadian infrastructure is undergoing a radical, state-driven transformation in 2026.The Quebec Hydro Rate Shock and the Erosion of ArbitrageHistorically, Canada's primary competitive advantage in global datacenter hosting was rooted in the province of Quebec's abundant, exceptionally low-cost, and sustainable hydroelectric power. However, the explosive, exponential growth of artificial intelligence has fundamentally altered grid dynamics. Global AI power capacity escalated to approximately 30 GW by late 2025, and Hydro-Québec internal projections indicate that datacenter electricity demand within the province will surge an astonishing sevenfold by 2035, exceeding 1,000 MW.   To prevent these colossal fixed infrastructure and grid expansion costs from being passed onto regular residential consumers—and to politically and economically extract the "full value" of the province's green energy premium—Hydro-Québec formally petitioned the provincial energy regulator (Régie de l'énergie) to double the electricity rates for new data centers exceeding 5 MW of capacity. Set to take effect in the second half of 2026, the new rate establishes a strict baseline of 13 cents per kilowatt-hour (kWh) for large AI data centers, while cryptographic and blockchain operations face an even steeper, punitive hike to an average of 19.5 cents/kWh.   This 13¢/kWh rate fundamentally reshapes the cross-border cost comparison. While it remains nominally lower than the United States national commercial average of 14.12¢/kWh, it severely erodes the historic energy arbitrage advantage against prime US datacenter hubs. For instance, Virginia—the densest and most mature datacenter market in the world—maintains average commercial electricity rates of approximately 11.44¢ to 13.5¢/kWh depending on specific load tiering, while Texas operates at highly competitive commercial rates near 10.7¢/kWh. By achieving cost-parity with the absolute core of the United States infrastructure market, Quebec's sovereign compute clusters can no longer compete strictly on lower operating margins. They must now justify their premium pricing through enhanced privacy, rigid security protocols, and ironclad jurisdictional guarantees.   Comparative Electricity Costs: Sovereign vs. Hyperscale HubsThe impact of grid economics can be quantified by comparing the prevailing rates in major geographic hosting zones.
